data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Tariff Risk Largely Mitigated By Crashing Canadian Dollar
data.description:
Markets sold off last week after Trump confirmed his intention to move forward with 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada, which will go into effect on March 4th. Of course, as we saw last month, we cannot know whether this will truly go into effect until then. However, his administration has made it clear that it does not feel the two countries are doing enough to secure the border.

In my view, the most important outcome of these tariffs will be their impact on the energy market. Trump intends to place a lower 10% tax on Canadian energy imports, which will not have the same impact as a 25% tariff but is still likely to upset the energy market. This tariff is expected to raise prices for US refineries and potentially lower refinery output in midwestern states. Canada accounts for just over half of all US oil imports.

The key issue is the regional impact, as the US does not have sufficient oil pipelines and transportation capacity to move oil from producing areas to midwestern refineries. Unlike coastal refineries, these refineries depend on Canadian oil pipelines and cannot switch sources. Further, around 97% of Canadian oil exports go to the US, so Canada cannot easily switch its export location without a massive infrastructure overhaul.

In theory, tariffs should encourage Canadian exporters and US midstream companies to divert toward the Midwest. However, there is no immediate free market for oil because of immense physical infrastructure and engineering barriers to shifting buyers and sellers. Creating pipelines to untangle the US-Canadian oil trade will take many years and significant capital investment. Thus, it is unclear who the end payee of the Canadian oil tariff will be. Refineries in the Midwest may opt to produce less due to higher costs, likely pushing gasoline prices up in those states to meet the added cost. Thus, Canadian oil producers may see slightly lower sales, while many US consumers may see higher gasoline prices.

We still do not know if these tariffs will go into effect. Given their objective economic inefficiency, at least until new pipelines and transportation investments can be made, it is also unclear how long these tariffs will last. Still, certain Canadian companies, such as Canadian Natural Resources (NYSE:CNQ), are likely to be impacted. CNQ is currently down around 9% this year, while the US Energy Sector ETF (XLE) is up about 7%. This implies the market is reacting to the news by expecting lower profits for Canadian Natural Resources and higher profits for US energy companies. US energy companies generally should benefit from higher product prices via domestic production protection.

I last had a bullish outlook on CNQ last February because, at that time, it seemed North American producers had a distinct edge in light of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East. I shifted toward a neutral view in the June article "Canadian Natural Resources: Rising Premium To Peer Group Points To Lower Relative Performance," warning that the company's valuation premium was excessive. CNQ has since fallen by 20% as its valuation premium has closed, oil prices have failed to rise, and it faces added risk due to tariffs. Given this, I believe it is an excellent time to reconsider CNQ's profit and valuation potential.

CNQ's Tariff Risk is Currency Dependent

In general, because the US produces oil domestically and imports from many countries, Canadian producers will likely see lower sales to the US if 10% tariffs go into effect. Although a 10% oil and gas tariff is not massive, buyers will always look for the lowest price, which will not be on Canadian oil. In January, Alberta's Premier warned that Canada must prepare for energy tariffs, relating that her conversation with Trump implied the tariffs would go into effect.

Canadian Natural Resources was already in a slightly challenged position. In October, the company posted strong Q3 earnings but said it would reduce natural gas grilling due to low prices, lowering its overall well-completion outlook. That reflects the general trend in the US, with American energy companies maintaining a lower rig count due to oil being at the breakeven price for newly drilled wells. The Canadian rig count is more volatile but has been stronger, potentially due to exceptionally high US demand for Canadian oil. See below:

Canadian Natural Resources is a major benefactor of the low CAD-USD exchange rate. To a large degree, the decline in the Canadian dollar may offset the tariff risk, mainly if the Canadian dollar sells off more in response to lower demand from tariffs. US demand for Canadian goods will decline due to 25% tariffs, likely pushing the currency's trade value lower. However, I do not expect demand for Canadian oil and natural gas to fall by the same degree, due to lower tariffs and the fact the US is mainly dependent on these imports. Thus, there is some potential that CNQ's sales will not be harmed as the CAD may fall by an additional 10%.

As you can see below, CNQ's profit increase since 2020 is partially driven by a lower CAD-USD:

CNQ is doing business in Canadian dollars, and declines in the Canadian dollar have not yet resulted in an inflation spike that would disproportionately raise its operating costs. Canada's YoY inflation rate is low at 1.9%, while its unemployment rate has risen to 6.7%, potentially signaling a high recession risk. Theoretically, a recession may benefit CNQ by lowering the CAD-USD exchange rate and lowering wage and capital cost inflation, which has been high in recent years.

Overall, I think investors and analysts should account for CNQ's tariff risk, but understand that exchange rate impacts heavily offset it. We generally see a stronger US dollar in response to tariffs, as they should lower US demand for other currencies. This is particularly true for highly impacted currencies like CAD-USD, which have already fallen over the past year to benefit exporters like CNQ. Although the US dollar may eventually reverse if tariffs harm the domestic economy or result in hefty reciprocal tariffs, I generally expect the energy tariff burden to fall on Canadian importers, who will have lower trade power. CNQ's sales to the US may slip, but I expect the result to be only around 1-3%, given the anticipated impact on exchange rates.

What is Canadian Natural Resources Worth?

The company's Q4 results will be released on March 6th. Analysts expect an EPS of $0.67, down 22% year over year. The consensus expects EPS to be around $2.5-$2.7 over the coming years before potentially rebounding in 2026. Given the lack of change in oil prices, these assumptions are reasonable. Tariffs may cause negative changes, but that is exchange rate dependent.

The rise of US energy stocks over recent months while Canadian counterparts decline has largely closed CNQ's valuation premium. CNQ's forward "P/E" of 11.1X is just below the sector median at 11.5X. Its forward "EV/EBITDA" of 5.6X is significantly below the sector median of 6.6X. CNQ's dividend yield of 5.5% is also well above the sector median of 4.2%.

The company's production is very balanced between natural gas (27%), heavy oil (26%), light oil (11%), and synthetic crude (36%). Its natural gas segment was an area of weakness in 2024, but its prospects have improved, given Henry Hub is now back to about $3.9/MMBTU due to cooler winter weather and stagnant North American output. Across the industry, capital investments into drilling have been low since ~2023, potentially creating a bullish long-term opportunity for energy prices.

Overall, I think CNQ is fairly valued today and potentially undervalued. Canadian Natural Resources has a lower valuation than most energy firms, with a particularly high dividend and lower debt leverage. Its debt-to-EBITDA ratio is only around 0.65X. The company has wisely lowered investments during the current lower energy commodity price market while pursuing strategic asset swaps with peers (SHEL) to raise output more capital-efficiently.

Although tensions in the Middle East have quieted in recent months, I still see CNQ as a hidden hedge against this issue. In my view, until there is true peace in that region, investors should be aware of the risk that oil prices skyrocket if a conflict impairs Iranian/Saudi energy exports, as well as that of their allies. If this occurs, and a large portion of global energy exports are threatened, oil could rise astronomically, giving immense benefits to North American producers to fill the gap. My view is speculative, and this may hopefully never occur, but I think CNQ is a good way to hedge against this significant portfolio risk.

The Bottom Line

I believe CNQ's negative trend regarding tariff concerns may be a "sell the rumor, buy the news" event. In other words, I think the profit risk of tariffs is generally overstated, given how low the Canadian dollar is in response to that country's economic woes. If 25% tariffs go into effect, I expect the CAD will crash by more than 10%, keeping Canadian oil and gas as competitive as they are today.

However, I will change my view if the US dollar declines, likely requiring a fall in the US economic outlook, which is currently stable. Last week's market declines were shared by stocks and oil, implying that the US economy may be slowing. This could harm domestic oil and gas demand, lowering energy companies' profits.

Accordingly, CNQ faces notable risks and uncertainties today that could cause the stock to fall further. Although I am slightly bullish on it, due mainly to its lower valuation, higher dividend, and my positive views on its management team, I would not be surprised to see CNQ continue to decline over the coming weeks and months. Still, to me, declines in CNQ are a buying opportunity for long-term investors. Further, although I generally expect tariffs to go into effect, I do not currently expect them to last for energy, given they're primarily a "lose-lose" situation for both the US and Canadian economies. If Trump decides to forgo energy tariffs, that could undo CNQ's recent declines.

data.title: ADT Inc.: Earnings Growth Will Drive Share Price Upside
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about ADT Inc. (ADT) previously with a buy rating as the path forward for growth to accelerate was positive in my view. 4Q24 earnings showed the strength of fundamentals, as seen from ADT’s key operating metrics. ADT is also executing well on its ADT+ platform and insurance integrations. I expect ADT to deliver management’s guided FY25 EPS growth and see earnings growth accelerate to low-teens percentage as consensus has expected.

4Q24 earnings showed the fundamental strength of ADT

ADT did well for 4Q24. Total revenue grew to $1.26 billion from $1.172 billion last year. While adj EBITDA margin was lower by ~100bps (down to 52%), absolute adj EBITDA was up from $627 million to $653 million. For FY25, management guided for revenue in the range of $5.025 billion to $5.225 billion, adj EBITDA in the range of $2.65 to $2.75 billion, and adj EPS of $0.77 to $0.85.

I believe the focus for 4Q24 results is beyond the headline figures. ADT’s key operating metrics really showcased how strong ADT’s business is. ADT remains as a business with highly predictable subscription revenue. There are four highlights to note:

  1. The 2% y/y increase in recurring monthly revenue [RMR] to $359 million;
  2. Revenue payback period remained steady at ~2.2 years, a sign that its customer acquisition strategy remains efficient (i.e. it has not reached a stage where marginal returns start to decay strongly);
  3. ADT’s churn reduction efforts are gaining traction – last twelve months [LTM] gross customer revenue attrition came down 20bps y/y to 12.7% y/y. This tells me that ADT strategic shift toward professionally installed systems, combined with better virtual service capabilities, is improving retention.
  4. Lastly, ADT also showed that it has strong pricing power, evident from higher average price realization across monitoring services and professional installations.

ADT+ Adoption Is Solid

ADT’s transition to its proprietary ADT+ platform is also starting to show tangible benefits. Take, for instance, in 4Q24, installation revenue surged 48% y/y to $175 million, clearly reflecting a greater mix of professionally installed systems. Feedback has been positive, and this is an important datapoint to note. As per the transcript, since its national rollout, the platform has maintained an average 4.8-star rating on the Apple App Store and Google Play. I also like that ADT is innovating new features that have resonated with customers. For instance, features like Trusted Neighbor allows users to grant secure access to their homes remotely.

In my opinion, this not only helps drive growth (professionally installed systems have higher revenue per user), it also enhances ADT’s revenue structure because customers purchase equipment upfront. Historically, ADT’s revenue was heavily dependent on acquiring new customers to sustain growth, as the bulk of its business came from recurring monitoring fees. This meant that aggressive customer acquisition efforts were necessary to offset churn and drive revenue growth. However, with ADT+, customers are required to buy Google Nest-integrated equipment upfront as part of a professional installation package, which means a larger portion of ADT’s revenue coming from one-time installation fees rather than purely from ongoing subscriptions. This shift is particularly important because it reduces ADT’s reliance on constantly acquiring new customers to drive revenue growth. You can think of this as a new revenue stream that provides cushion for revenue growth volatility.

State Farm Partnership Channel Momentum continues

ADT’s strategic alliance with State Farm also continues to gain momentum, showing its potential to become a meaningful growth driver. Sales through the State Farm channel reached 18,000 in 2024, up from 5,500 in 2023, reflecting strong demand from homeowners looking to bundle security solutions with their insurance policies. The partnership has now expanded to 17 states, covering ~45% of State Farm’s home policies, with additional pilots underway. I reiterate that the long-term potential here is significant. State Farm’s scale gives ADT a direct pathway to reach millions of customers who might not otherwise consider professional security solutions. Moreover, the introduction of water-leak detection features in select states should enable ADT to cross-sell more products. My view is that so long as ADT can continue to deepen its insurance ecosystem integrations, it will become a strong long-term catalyst for revenue growth.

AI and Virtual Service Supports Margin Expansion

Given that every other company is talking about AI, I should also point out that ADT is benefiting from this AI trend. More specifically, ADT’s investments in AI-driven automation are beginning to pay off, both in terms of cost reduction and customer experience. In 4Q24, AI-powered virtual agents handled 50% of customer service chats, and ADT is targeting this to increase to 90% by 1Q25. Additionally, management is targeting 20% of all voice calls to be handled by AI by year-end. This is a huge step up in automation, and I would imagine this to directly translate into cost savings. My logic is that by automating customer interactions, ADT reduces its reliance on human agents, which means lower labor costs, while maintaining service quality. ADT AI efforts extend beyond just customer service agents, it has also showcased its ability to leverage AI to improve its remote diagnostic capabilities. For those that have missed this data point, ADT now resolves 50% of service issues virtually. You can totally imagine how much cost ADT has saved from reduced deployment of technicians to on-site visits.

Valuation

Management's FY25 guidance calls for 8% EPS growth, which I think sets the baseline to think about ADT future EPS growth. Remember that the ADT+ platform adoption is still at the early phase of ramping upwards and there is still plenty of room for ADT to penetrate in the State Farm partnership. Also, margins should see a structural step up as ADT continues its automation efforts. Consensus FY26 estimate is for adj EPS to reach $0.93 (from adj EPS of $0.75 in FY24), implying ~12%/year adj EPS growth. I think this is a fair estimate given my thoughts on the ADT business as discussed above, and if ADT delivers the expected earnings growth, the stock should be worth ~$9.3 (~15% upside from current share price) assuming the current 10x forward PE valuation. The upside case is that the market attaches a higher multiple because ADT growth and margin profile are structurally better than in the past. If we assume that ADT trades back to its past 3 years average – 12.5x – the upside is even more attractive.

Risk

Fewer home relocations due to rates staying higher for longer could limit new sign-ups. Tariff-related cost increases on equipment sourced from Mexico could also negatively impact margin as subscriber acquisition costs go up. Additionally, no bulk account purchases were made in 4Q24, and none are planned for 2025. This could cause FY25 growth to seem weak, potentially impacting the stock’s sentiment.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for ADT, as I believe it remains well positioned for continued growth, supported by ADT+ adoption and its insurance partnerships. ADT’s AI-driven initiatives should also help it to improve margins structurally. With shares trading at ~10x forward earnings, I see a clear path to 15% upside or more.

data.title: QQQX: 8%-Plus Yield, 9% Discount, Long-Term NAV Growth
data.description:
In Friday's article, we profiled the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), a closed end fund, "CEF", which sells covered call options on NASDAQ stocks, in order to create income for its investors.

This article covers one of JEPQ's competitors, the Nuveen NASDAQ 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (NASDAQ:QQQX), another CEF which writes covered calls on the NASDAQ 100.

Fund Profile:

QQQX "is designed to offer regular distributions through a strategy that seeks attractive total return with less volatility than the Nasdaq 100 Index by investing in a U.S. equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movements of the Nasdaq 100 Index, as well as selling call options on 35%-75% of the notional value of the Fund's equity portfolio (with a 55% long-term target) in an effort to enhance the Fund's risk-adjusted returns. The strategy will consider the Fund's tax position and employ techniques to improve after-tax shareholder outcomes." (QQQX site)

QQQX has $1.38B in assets, with 284 holdings, an average daily volume of 128K, and an expense ratio of 0.92%. As of 1/31/25, 66% of its holdings were overwritten with call options. It began in January 2007.

Dividends:

QQQX paid a higher quarterly distribution in December '24 of $.56, up from $.42, its steady quarterly dividend since Q1 '23. Based upon its 2/28/25 closing price of $25.91, and its latest distribution of $.56, QQQX's forward yield is 8.65%, whereas its trailing 12-month yield is 7.10%.

QQQX's overall distribution schedule is March/June/Sept./Dec. It generally goes ex-dividend in the middle of the month, and pays at the end of the month, or in the 1st week of the following month.

QQQX's most recent financial report, (Q1-2 '24), showed negative NII of $202K, with $16.15M in net realized gains, vs. $41M in dividends paid. Unrealized appreciation was $163M, up 7% on an annualized basis vs. 2023's total of $284M.

NII and net realized gains both moved at a slower pace in Q1-2 '24, than in full year 2023, when NII was -$113K, and net realized gains were $43.5M.

Taxes:

2024 distributions were estimated to have been comprised of 39.6% in Long-Term gains, 60.4% in Return of Capital, "ROC". ROC offers investors a tax deferral advantage, but it does decrease your tax basis, which will impact your capital gains if you sell your shares.

Holdings:

3 out of QQQX's top 5 industries are tech-related, Semiconductors, Software, and Tech Hardware, Storage & Peripherals, combining for 49.5% of its portfolio, a bit underweight vs. the NASDAQ 100's 58.69% weighting.

It also holds 13.6% in Interactive Media & Services, via its holdings of Meta, and Alphabet. Broadline Retail accounted for 6.2% of its holdings, with 5.9% in Amazon, as of 1/31/25.

Like JEPQ, QQQX's top 10 positions include the so-called Magnificent Seven stocks - Apple, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Nvidia, Microsoft, and Tesla, in addition to Cisco and Broadcom. The top 10 comprises ~57% of QQQX's portfolio.

Performance:

QQQX's price performance since inception averaged 10.58%, vs. NAV performance of 11.09%, as of 1/31/25. In general, its price performance has lagged its NAV performance, indicating discounted prices to NAV over the long run.

Its 3-year average shows the biggest discount, with an 8.06% price performance, vs. a 10.26% NAV performance.

QQQX outperformed JEPQ, but lagged the NASDAQ 100 in price during 2024, due to its covered call strategy, which caps upside price gain participation. Its better price performance, and 7.87% yield put its 2024 total return closer of 24.87% closer to QQQ's 25.54% return, vs. 23.48% for JEPQ.

2023 was the opposite, with newish fund JEPQ outperforming QQQX in price by over 800 basis points, and QQQX's total 2023 return of 21.39% lagging JEPQ's 33.65% 2023 return by over 1200 basis points. Both funds lagged the NASDAQ 100 by a wide margin in 2023.

Risks:

Price Risk: CEF's often trade at a discount, which exposes investors to loss of capital. As you'll see in the valuations section, one way to try to avoid that risk is to attempt to buy CEF's at deeper than historical average discounts.

Call Option Risk: Selling calls generates income, but your upside price participation is limited by the strike price of the sold call option. The flip side of that is that the sold call option premiums offer you a buffer in a down market.

Common Stock Price Risk: The underlying equities' prices may fluctuate quickly - for example, look at Tesla's 27% drop so far in 2025. JEPQ is down 4.2% so far in 2025, and most of its top 10 holdings have also declined in price, with the exceptions of Meta and CSCO:

Valuations:

Buying CEF's at a deeper discount than their historical average discounts/premiums can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. Since NAV/Share is calculated at the end of each trading day, you have to look at the most recent closing values to determine the current NAV discount or premium.

At its 2/27/25 closing price of $25.73, QQQX was selling at a 9.11% discount to its NAV/share of $28.11. While that's not as deep as its 1-year average discount of 9.85%, it's a lot deeper than its 3-year average discount of 2.66%, and its 5-year average discount of 1.82%.

Parting Thoughts:

We rate QQQX as a speculative Buy, it offers an attractive 8%-plus yield, and has a good long-term track record. Unlike many CEF's, QQQX has increased its NAV/share since inception, growing it by 35%, from $19.10 to $25.73.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: The Brink's Company: Path To High Single Digits Growth Remains Intact
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on The Brink’s Company (BCO) was a buy rating because I remained confident that BCO would see growth accelerate to high-single-digit percentages, backed by solid adoption trends seen in the Digital Retail Solutions [DRS] and ATM Managed Services [AMS] segments. I reiterate my buy rating, as I do not see any structural changes to BCO’s ability to accelerate growth.

4Q24 results update

BCO 4Q24 results suggested a mixed headline growth performance, but I believe it showed strong underlying momentum. In 4Q, BCO reported revenue of $1.26 billion, increasing 1.5% y/y. While reported growth seemed weak, organic revenue growth, however, was much stronger at 10.7% y/y, supported by continued momentum in AMS and DRS, which grew 23% y/y. Regionally, every region saw positive growth: North America saw 3% organic revenue growth; Latin America saw 29.4%; Europe saw 5.8% y/y, and the rest of the world saw 1.5% y/y. From a business mix perspective, AMS and DRS now contribute 24% of total revenue on a trailing 12-month [TTM] basis. For profitability, adj EBITDA margin sustained at the ~20% range, coming in at $251 million, and EPS saw $2.12.

AMS and DRS Are Growth Engines

In my view, the key focus for BCO results should be the key growth drivers: AMS and DRS. These segments continue to grow much faster than the rest of the business, growing 23% y/y in 4Q24 and contributing ~$200 million in incremental revenue for the full year. AMS, in particular, has seen a 20% increase in installed devices in FY24, while DRS is benefiting from accelerated rollouts in multiple international markets. If you do the math, AMS and DRS are 24% of total revenue and are growing >20%, which equates to ~5% growth on a consolidated basis.

These segments are not seeing growth slowdown, and I don’t expect growth to slow down given the runway for adoption is still long. The demand for outsourced ATM and retail cash management services remains significant, especially outside of North America. The main underlying driver is that financial institutions [FI], in my opinion, will continue to find ways to reduce costs and improve operational efficiency (these moves by HSBC, Standard Chartered, and Citigroup are clear signs that FI are in cost-optimization mode). One of the most labor-intensive aspects of the banking business is managing cash (this dated article also discussed what I mentioned), and hence, I remained confident that BCO can capitalize on this trend.

As such, I am confident that BCO can achieve the management target that AMS and DRS can maintain double-digit growth, and this should help drive BCO growth back to the high-single-digit range. Additionally, given these are higher-margin businesses, I also see this as a key driver to EBITDA margin expansion over the next few years.

CVM Remains a Cash Generator, Brink’s Global Services Shows Signs of Rebound

Even in BCO’s legacy business—Cash & Valuables Management [CVM]—growth was a lot better than I expected, growing 7% y/y in 4Q24, supported by both pricing and volume growth. What’s interesting here is the traction seen in Brink’s Global Services [BGS]. After a tough few quarters, BGS returned to positive y/y growth in 4Q24, and I expect this momentum to continue into early 2025, driven by higher movements of precious metals like gold and silver into the US. Given the uncertainty in how the USD will react to Trump’s tariff policies, I think this positive demand momentum should continue for the near term due to currency hedging strategies.

Operational Efficiencies and Margin Expansion in Focus

Moving down BCO’s P&L, I believe focus should also be given to how well BCO is making steady progress in improving profitability. While 4Q24 adj EBITDA margin was modestly lower than 4Q23, on an FY24 basis, adj EBITDA margin expanded 40 bps y/y to 18.2%, and I do expect more expansion in the coming years. Apart from higher margin contribution from AMS and DRS, Brink’s Business System [BBS] —BCO’s operational framework—is also a driver. BCO transition toward a more standardized operating model should lead to lower service costs, which means better unit economics. As BCO steps up automation efforts in money processing and benefits from the shift toward a greater mix of AMS/DRS revenue, EBITDA margins should continue to expand in FY25, bringing BCO closer to its 20% medium-term target.

Valuation

I believe the upside remains very attractive. The market does not seem to give credit to the higher growth segments and margin expansion runway. I reiterate my growth assumption that BCO should see growth accelerate to high single-digit percentages in FY27. However, as for margin expansion, given how FY24 has performed, I now see a path for BCO to achieve higher than FY23 margins if margins can continue to expand at ~25bps/year (my previous assumption, which is not a high hurdle given that management guided for FY25 adj EBITDA margin to expand by at least 30bps). If BCO fundamentals perform as I expected, even if multiples do not re-rate higher (stay at the current 12.5% forward P/E), the upside is attractive enough to warrant a buy rating.

Risk

I think the key risk for BCO is still FX fluctuations, in particular, currency devaluations in Argentina, Mexico, and Brazil. Additionally, further regulatory scrutiny could result in more one-off charges (BCO recently settled with the Department of Justice and Financial Crimes Enforcement Network). Lastly, the gradual decline in cash usage, particularly in developed markets, remains a long-term structural risk.

Conclusion

My view for BCO remains a buy rating as the fundamental growth story remains intact. AMS and DRS continue to grow robustly, and I do not see structural headwinds that would impact these segments growth. The long-term trend of FI cutting costs and optimizing cash management continues to benefit BCO. At the current valuation, so long as BCO can grow as I expected, the upside is very attractive, in my opinion.

data.title: Radiant Logistics: Fair Value With M&A Opportunities To Boost Long-Term Growth
data.description:
In November 2024, I marked Radiant Logistics (NYSE:RLGT) a high-risk buy opportunity. The reason to mark the risk was the fact that 2025 earnings were already priced in. Furthermore, the margins in the business are thin. That is not only the case for Radiant Logistics but also for its peers.

Since my buy rating, the stock price has decreased nearly 10%. The stock is still up 8.5% since my first buy rating and is also still showing market outperforming returns. However, in a renewed risk environment, one can wonder how long that outperformance will hold.

In this report, I will update the risk assessment, discuss the most recent earnings and update my price target and rating.

Trade War Casts Shadow On Freight And Logistics

As I pointed out in my risk assessment in August, the risk for Radiant Logistics is any softness in the macroeconomic environment. The company is able to take out some variable costs when market conditions worsen, but even in good times the margins are thin.

Currently, we are seeing that risk is inflating. This is due to a global trade war between the US and its biggest trading partners. President Trump has shared that tariffs against Mexico and Canada will be instated on the 4th of March. Furthermore, additional tariffs against Chinese goods are on the table.

The problem I see with tariffs is that they eventually hurt the consumer and consumer spending might be dented. Consequently, this means that demand for products with or without tariffs declines and freight and logistics service providers have to scale accordingly.

For a business that is already not extremely profitable from a margin perspective, that is a major risk. During the second quarter, 10.5% of the sales of Radiant Logistics were generated from the Canadian activities. In the case of tariffs being implemented, it is not just those revenues that are at risk. The way things seem to be heading now, the global network could be plagued by the consequences of a trade war. Trade wars are rarely isolated events and have widespread consequences.

The opportunities for Radiant Logistics are obviously a stabilization in the demand-supply environment, but also include M&A activity to increase market share and as much as possible to amortize costs. That has worked well for Radiant Logistics as it grew with EBITDA growth outpacing sales growth. Furthermore, it can convert some agent stations into company-owned stations, which could be beneficial to margins.

Radiant Logistics Profits Increase

Second quarter sales increased 31.5% to $264.5 million. The increase was driven by growth in project charters and to a lesser extent through acquisitions. Profits increased from $1.66 million to $8.7 million

What we see in the results is the cost coupling with higher revenues. Cost of transportation service grew more or less in line with revenues in absolute sense. The profit increase was primarily driven by lower operating partner commissions. This demonstrates the margin additions Radiant can achieve through conversions of agent stations.

Margins increased by 0.8% to 3.2%. The margin jump was significant. However, it was almost fully realized by the commission savings. The variable cost component on transportation grew from 69% of sales to 76% of sales. To me, this shows high costs of transportation and the significant cost inflation that occurs when revenues increase.

Radiant Logistics Stock Is Fairly Valued

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Wall Street analysts have a $9.75 price target for Radiant Logistics with a strong buy rating. I do not share that optimism. In my view, the stock is currently fairly valued based on the company’s median EV/EBITDA.

Compared to my prior assessment, I have revised the price target down from $9.41 to $8.46. This is driven by the assumption that the company will continue to pursue M&A opportunities. In case there will be macroeconomic pressure, those acquisitions may not be flat growth on top of the current estimates.

However, I am maintaining my buy rating for the simple reason that the company has a good track record on M&A integration.

Conclusion: Radiant Logistics Sees Elevated Risk

I believe that Radiant Logistics is currently experiencing elevated risks driven by the uncertainty of trade. We will have to see how things will develop over time, but the prospect of widespread tariffs is not a great outlook for any company involved in freight and logistics.

Radiant Logistics, however, could benefit in the sense that the elevated risk may reduce its acquisition costs and in fact boost M&A opportunities. These opportunities may not necessarily layer in positively from the start, but strengthen the business for the longer term. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating.

data.title: REITs Rally As Rates Tumble
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

US equity markets declined for the fourth week in the past five - while benchmark interest rates tumbled to two-month lows - on a frenetic week of geopolitical headlines and economic data indicating a sluggish start to 2025. The Atlanta Fed's updated GDPNow forecast indicated a -1.5% contraction in first-quarter U.S. economic growth - down sharply from +2.3% in the prior week. While much of this plunge was attributable to a surge in net imports as retailers stockpiled goods ahead of anticipated tariffs, there were some evident "cracks in the consumer" in PCE data, which showed the first decline in personal spending in two years, consistent with recent survey data showing a dip in consumer and business sentiment in January and early February.

Following a decline of 1.6% in the prior week, the S&P 500 declined another 1.0% on the week, requiring a Friday late-afternoon rally to avoid posting its worst week since September. Mega-cap technology stocks - which have fueled the broader market gains over the past three years - were slammed this week after earnings results from AI stalwart Nvidia fell shy of high expectations. Erasing its year-to-date gains, the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 dipped 3.4% on the week, following declines of 2% last week. The Mid-Cap 400 - comprised largely of domestic-focused industry groups - was the notable outperformer among the major benchmarks. Buoyed by the continued interest rate retreat, real estate equities were also a notable source of strength, as REIT earnings season wrapped up with a surprisingly solid slate of reports. The Equity REIT Index rallied 2.5% on the week, with 14-of-18 property sectors in positive territory, while the Mortgage REIT Index advanced 2.4%. Homebuilders rebounded as soft housing market data was offset by mortgage rate relief.

Closing out the week at its lowest level since early December, the 10-Year Treasury Yield tumbled 22 basis points on the week to close at 4.21% - well below its recent peak last month of 4.80%, but still up sharply from its mid-September lows of 3.62%. The 2-Year Treasury Yield also tumbled 21 basis points to 3.98% - down from its recent peak of 4.40%, but still up from its mid-September closing low of 3.55%. Swaps markets now imply a Fed Funds Rate of 3.91% at the end of 2025 (vs. 4.50% now), implying 2.3 Fed interest rate cuts in 2025 - a more dovish outlook compared to the 1.9 cuts implied last week - but still far more hawkish than the 5.2 expected at "peak dovishness" last September. Markets now price-in 82% odds of a rate cut by June - up from odds of around 25% in mid-February. Helping the inflation and rate outlook, Commodities prices plunged nearly 4% on the week, while WTI Crude Oil prices posted its lowest end-of-week close since December as traders monitored the status of tariff negotiations with Mexico and Canada, and digested the implications of a fiery Oval Office meeting with Ukraine.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important economic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

One week after the Citi Economic Surprise Index dipped into negative territory for the first time since September, investors parsed another series of weaker-than-expected reports this week, showing that the "bad vibes" picked-up in recent sentiment data has indeed translated into lethargic economic activity, accompanied by the "silver lining" of cooling inflation. The Personal Income and Outlays report underscored this good news/bad news dynamic: Personal Spending - a broad measure of consumer spending - posted a monthly decline for the first time since March 2023, but the Core PCE Price Index - the Federal Reserve's preferred gauge of inflation - posted its lowest annual increase since March 2021. Meanwhile, the Atlanta Fed turned heads this week after its updated GDPNow tracker showed that that GDP is now pacing to contract 1.5% in the first quarter - down sharply from the 2.3% growth expected in the prior reading earlier in the week. The sharp revision was fueled primarily by trade data showing that retailers loaded-up on inventory at a historic pace in January to front-run pending tariffs on China, Mexico, and Canada. Imports are expected to detract -3.7 percentage points from Q1 GDP, although this would effectively be added back in Q2 as activity normalizes.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Following a jam-packed slate of 50 REIT earnings reports this week, we're now effectively complete with real estate earnings season. As discussed in our Earnings Scorecard report, this earnings season was one of the strongest in the past half-decade, with roughly two-thirds of REITs topping estimates. Of the 95 equity REITs that provide full-year guidance for Funds from Operations ("FFO"), 63 (66%) beat their most recent full-year outlook, while just 9 (9%) missed their outlook - well above the historical average "beat rate" for the fourth quarter of roughly 55%. Perhaps more importantly, the outlook for 2025 indicated that the "real estate bear market" dating back to the start of the Fed's rate hiking cycle in March 2022 has finally run its course. Despite the lingering interest rate headwinds, REITs are forecast to deliver 3% FFO growth in 2025, up from growth of around 2% in 2023 and 2024. Senior Housing REITs were the "star of the show" this earnings season, while results from other residential REIT sectors this week indicated a reacceleration in rent growth in 2025 after three years of deceleration. While there were no major sector-level "bombshells" this earnings season, results from Hotel REITs were mildly disappointing as margin pressures from higher labor costs offset impressive demand. Below, we discuss the upside and downside highlights of the past week of REIT earnings results, with extended analysis later this weekend in our Winners and Losers of REIT Earnings season report.

Manufactured Housing: Beginning with the upside standouts this week, Sun Communities (SUI) - the largest manufactured housing ("MH") REIT - rallied 8% after reporting solid results and announcing a deal to sell its Safe Harbor marinas business to private equity giant Blackstone (BX) for $5.65B in an all-cash transaction. Sun initially purchased Safe Harbor - the largest marina owner in the U.S. - in September 2020 for $2.1B. SUI has grown the business from 101 marinas in 2020 to 138 today. This marina segment comprised roughly a quarter of SUI's annual net operating income, and has performed relatively well since its acquisition, with average annual NOI growth of around 6% - well above that of its struggling RV segment and roughly matching the performance of its core manufactured housing segment. The purchase price represents a 21x multiple on 2024 FFO - a premium to SUI's current FFO multiple of roughly 19x - and is expected to generate $5.5B of pretax proceeds after transaction costs. SUI - which was the strongest-performing REIT in the 2010s - has come under pressure in recent years as its ancillary business lines - RV parks, Marinas, and a UK portfolio - have generally underperformed its core MH portfolio. Sun forecasted a return to FFO growth in 2025 after two negative years, fueled by impressive 6% NOI growth in its core MH segment and a return to growth for its long-lagging RV segment.

Healthcare: Embattled hospital owner Medical Properties Trust (MPW) surged 20% this week - extending its rally since early February to over 50% - after reporting some success in re-tenanting the properties formerly operated by bankrupt tenants Steward Healthcare and Prospect Medical. Consistent with the positive healthcare industry trends seen in the skilled nursing and senior housing space this earnings season, MPW noted an improvement in the financial health of its hospital operators, citing a recovery in admissions and surgical volumes alongside improving margins after several years of critical labor shortages, which helped to fuel an improvement in rent coverage. MPW reported full-year FFO of $0.80 - down roughly 50% from last year - but appears to have done enough on the capital-raising front to keep it afloat, with liquidity sufficient to satisfy its debt obligations up to October 2027. Elsewhere, this week, Medical office building REIT Global Medical (GMRE) - which also has exposure to some struggling hospital operators - rallied 5% after reporting solid results and forecasting a return to FFO growth in 2025. American Healthcare (AHR) - which was among the few REIT IPOs amid the three-year real estate "bear market" - rallied 9% on the week after its results showed similarly robust strength in its senior housing portfolio as its larger peers. AHR reported a staggering 53% NOI growth in its Senior Housing Operating Portfolio ("SHOP") segment in 2024, and expects 20% NOI growth in this SHOP segment in 2025, fueling a hearty 12% increase in FFO.

Net Lease: Underscoring the positive sentiment shift for REITs in recent weeks, even a REIT that announced a 30% dividend reduction and posted one of the few earnings "misses" still managed to post double-digit gains. Global Net Lease (GNL) - which had already sold nearly $1B in properties in 2024 as part of a "strategic disposition" strategy aimed at reducing debt - rallied more than 10% after announcing a $1.8B deal to sell its multi-tenant portfolio. The 100 property portfolio will be sold to RCG Ventures at an estimated 8.4% cash cap rate, a deal that GNL calls a "transformative transaction," which accelerates GNL's deleveraging and positions GNL as a pure-play, single-tenant net lease company. GNL simultaneously announced that it would reduce its dividend by 30% to $0.190/quarter starting in April 2025, but also approved a $300M share buyback program, which it plans to use opportunistically while targeting a year-end Debt-to-EBITDA multiple of 6.5x to 7.1x - roughly in-range with its larger net lease peers. Elsewhere, small-cap Postal Realty (PSTL) - which focuses exclusively on properties leased by the USPS - rallied 12% after reporting impressive results fueled by a combination of aggressive acquisitions and mid-single-digit rent growth. Citing success in negotiating annual escalations into the majority of its leases, PSTL posted sector-leading FFO growth of 8.4% in 2024 - significantly above consensus estimates - and projects similarly solid growth of over 4% in 2025

Self-Storage: Among the few property sectors on the downside this week, self-storage REITs slumped after results from each of the four REITs showed that sluggish property-level fundamentals lingered into early 2025, as the combination of decades-low housing market turnover and peaking new supply growth has sparked a rapid post-pandemic normalization in rental rates. Storage REITs - the leaders in FFO growth from 2019 through 2022 - expect a third-straight year of flat-to-negative FFO growth in 2025. Muted move-out activity - and relatively buoyant low-single-digit rent growth on existing tenants - helped to offset the considerable weakness in new lease rates, as all four storage REITs reported an eleventh-straight quarter of negative "Street Rate" (new lease) spreads, which has slowly started to bleed into the overall average rent metrics and into same-store NOI. Street Rates remained lower by an average of 10% in Q4 - which was actually the "least bad" since Q1 of 2023 - while average occupancy rates dipped below 90% for the first time in a decade. Extra Space (EXR) dipped 2% despite topping FFO estimates and reporting the lone increase in occupancy rates in the sector, as investors were spooked by a 9.5% jump in same-store expenses in Q4 driven by "outsized" increases in property taxes. National Storage (NSA) - which has lagged in recent years amid particularly sharp pressures on its Sunbelt markets - was the leader this week after calling a "bottom" in same-store performance.

Additional Headlines via The Daily REITBeat on iREIT+Hoya

  • AvalonBay (AVB) announced a $618M deal to acquire eight apartment communities from BSR Real Estate Investment Trust. AVB will pay $187M for two communities in Austin and $431.5 for six communities in Dallas in deals that are expected to close in Q2 2025.
  • In an NT 10-K filing, Crown Castle (CCI) announced that it will be late on its Annual Report due to "significant demands related to its strategic review" and notes that it "expects to record an impairment charge of approximately $5.0B related to the goodwill in its Fiber segment."

REIT Credit Ratings

  • S&P Global placed Sun Communities (SUI) on CreditWatch with positive implications, which reflects its view that leverage will be materially reduced after the Safe Harbors marina portfolio sale
  • S&P raised the credit ratings of Regency Centers (REG), including its issuer credit rating, to "A-" from “BBB+” with a stable outlook
  • S&P affirmed the credit ratings of Essential Properties (EPRT), including its "BBB-" issuer credit rating and revised its outlook to positive from stable
  • Fitch Ratings affirmed the credit ratings of Independence Realty (IRT), including the Issuer Default Rating at “BBB” with a stable outlook
  • Moody’s downgraded the credit ratings of Franklin Street (FSP), including its corporate rating to “Caa1” from “B3” but revised its outlook to stable from negative

2025 Performance & 2024 Recap

Through two months of 2025, the Equity REIT Index is now higher by 5.4% on the year, while the Mortgage REIT Index has advanced 11.0%, and the Housing 100 Index has gained 2.0%. This compares with the 1.3% gain for the S&P 500, the 0.7% decline for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 3.1% decline for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 14-of-18 property sectors are higher on the year, led by Healthcare, Industrial, and Mortgage REITs, while downside laggards have included Data Center, Hotel, and Office REITs. At 4.21%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now lower by 36 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield is lower by 26 basis points at 3.98%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is now higher by 1.5% this year on a total return basis, led by outperformance from long-duration Treasuries. In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil is lower by 0.3% this year, while the broader Commodities complex is higher by 9.9%. In currency markets, the US Dollar Index has declined 1.1% this year after appreciating 7% last year.

Looking back to 2024, we note that REITs surged 20% leading up to the Fed’s initial rate cut in September, but hit the skids after the rate cuts actually began. Including this past week, REITs have now underperformed the S&P 500 by roughly 50 percentage points since the start of the Fed hiking cycle in 2022 - a historically remarkable underperformance gap. For 2024, the Equity REIT Index posted modest total returns of 4.8%, while the Mortgage REIT Index posted total returns of 1.6% - significantly lagging the 26.2% gain on the S&P 500. Within the REIT sector, 12 of the 18 property sectors finished higher for the year, led by Data Center, Regional Mall, Healthcare, and Office REITs, while "goods-oriented" sectors generally lagged, with Timber, Industrial, and Cannabis REITs among the losers. Corresponding closely with movements in benchmark yields, Equity REITs were up by roughly 15% at their 2024 highs in early September prior to the Fed's rate cut, while Mortgage REITs were up by roughly 8% at their highs. At their lows in early April, both Equity and Mortgage REITs were down by roughly 10%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index ultimately posted modest total returns of roughly 1% on the year, while the Commodities complex gained about 6%.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

A critical slate of employment data highlights the economic calendar in the week ahead, with the main event coming on Friday via the BLS Nonfarm Payrolls. Economists expect job growth of 133k in February on the "headline" Establishment Survey, a modest cooldown in hiring following the 143k pace in January. The Average Hourly Earnings series within the payrolls report - the first major inflation print for February - is expected to show that annual wage growth remained at 4.1%. Earlier in the week, we'll see ADP Payrolls on Wednesday and Jobless Claims data on Thursday. The full slate of employment data will come alongside an active week of Federal Reserve commentary, highlighted by remarks on Friday from Fed Chair Powell at the Booth Monetary Policy Forum, which investors will be parsing for insights into the FOMC's updated monetary policy outlook following the wave of softer-than-expected economic data over the past month. We'll also see another flurry of business surveys, with S&P and ISM Manufacturing PMI data on Monday and Services PMI on Wednesday.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Center, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.

data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC: Dividend Cut As Expected, Risk Of Underperformance Still There
data.description:
My previous piece on Goldman Sachs BDC (NYSE:GSBD) was published back in December 2024 before the Q4 results were published. The thesis was very straightforward: to avoid GSBD because the fundamentals were so bad that the question about dividend cut was not "if", but "when". Plus, the P/NAV did not justify the embedded risks.

Since then, the total returns have landed at ~5%, which is not that bad, but certainly below the BDC index (BIZD). Yet, starting from my first bearish article on GSBD, the total return is negative 15%.

Another aspect, which happened together with the release of Q4 2024 report was ~29% dividend cut. I expected this to happen, but not as fast.

Currently, GSBD trades at a P/NAV of 0.95x, which is theoretically indicative of a sound business with just some marginal struggles.

Let's see whether this is really the case now after the Q4 2024 report and the recalibrated dividend.

Thesis review

When assessing BDCs I would say that adjusted NII per share is the most important metric as it captures the underlying cash flow generation level and allows to contextualize it with the dividends, net realized/unrealized gains etc. For example, if adjusted NII per share goes below the dividend, it means that a BDC has to use debt or sell off its assets to fund the gap.

Q4 2024 was very bad for GSBD in terms of the adjusted NII per share. The result came in at $0.47 per share, which is way below the previous quarter's figure of $0.57.

The key reasons for this were the following:

  • Lower asset base due to negative net funding volumes (i.e., loan maturities exceeding new commitments).
  • Lower yields stemming from reduced base rates.
  • Sector-wide spread compression, which reduces portfolio yields further.
  • Write-downs.

The dividend for Q4 2024 quarter remained unchanged at $0.45 per share, which, based on the recent results, translates to a dividend coverage of 104%.

So, it is quite logical that the Management decided to adjust the dividend to leave a higher margin of safety in the system.

The new base quarterly dividend is $0.32 per share. There is also a variable component attached to it that will be paid out depending on how GSBD performs. Here, the Management has already communicated that there will be a couple of $0.16 per share additional dividends paid on top of the base dividend in 2025.

All of this results in a total dividend of $0.48 per share, which is already below the Q4 2024 adjusted NII per share level.

In my opinion, it is a very aggressive move, and it is rather clear that after these supplementals are paid, the chances are extremely low that any new ones get announced.

I will give you four reasons why I think so.

First, in 2025 and 2026 GSBD will have to face additional pressures on the cash flows from higher costs of financing. As it can be seen in the table below, GSBD has two below market level borrowings, which after their refinancings will inflict damage on the interest cost item.

Second, the debt to equity stands at 1.23x, which is above sector average and provides no sustainable room for incremental borrowings that could be used to expand the portfolio and thus offset higher interest costs and yield compression.

Third, the portfolio companies are not the healthiest. For instance, the weighted average net leverage is 6.2x and the weighted average interest coverage is 1.8x. These levels are one of the weakest in the sector, and certainly not characteristic to BDCs, which trade close to their NAV.

Fourth, and this is very important, GSBD has a relatively notable part of total income that comes from PIK streams. PIK means that income is not earned via cash but instead with an increased loan position (principal). The general rule of the thumb is that PIK is riskier than pure first lien cash interest (e.g., companies might choose PIK because they do not have the ability to pay interest or BDCs are forced to transition to PIK to avoid direct non-accruals). In Q4 2024 the PIK consumed 15% of the total income generation.

The comment, however, by the Management (Alex Chi - Co-CEO & Co-President) provides some optimism, but I think that we can read through the lines that these are not the healthiest positions in GSBD's portfolio:

And if you look at the metrics such as leverage and interest coverage, we've seen some slight improvements in those as well. So I'd say, generally, credit health of the portfolio broadly is stable to improving. And the other thing I'd just point to is that where we see, PIK even in these instances of, either amendments or restructurings where there may be some liquidity shortfall at these portfolio companies or tight liquidity in the near term. Those are often accompanied by either, capital that the sponsor is putting in, or other concessions such as tighter covenants and things like that that we're getting. So we think we do have a tighter control over those portfolio companies as well.

The bottom line

Given the above and P/NAV of 0.95x, the answer for me is simple: there is no reason to buy GSBD. In fact, I would say that we will see a continued pressure on GSBD's price or at least an underperformance relative to the overall BDC sector.

Exhausted leverage profile, aggressive dividend policy (due to the supplementals), weak portfolio companies and expensive refinancings around the corner all bode ill for GSBD's fundamentals. In this context, P/NAV of 0.95x seems too low.

data.title: Nestle: New CEO's Plan, Even If Succeeds, Doesn't Equal Outperformance
data.description:
Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGY) is a consumer staples empire, with nearly 160 years of history, and several global brands we all love.

Just like many staple names, especially of this size, the growth story seems to have hit a wall.

Although many investors are drawn to the resiliency and safety of a company like Nestle, it's important to keep in mind that the path to market-beating returns remains highly uncertain.

Even if the new CEO's plan succeeds, I don't think it will yield the desired outcome.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To Nestle & Staples Saturation Problem

With over CHF 91 billion in annual sales, Nestle is the largest consumer staple company in the world, at least that I know of.

Across coffee, water, pet care, vitamins, ice cream, chocolates, and many more categories, Nestle owns a portfolio of global brands, including KitKat, Nespresso, Nesquik, Nestea, Haagen-Dazs, San Pellegrino, Perrier, and Purina.

The problem is that Nestle, alongside most of the companies in the sector, has pretty much saturated the market. They are already almost everywhere in the world, in every supermarket and every convenience store.

World population is growing, but not that fast, especially not in key markets. Meanwhile, up and coming smaller brands leveraging modern distribution, GLP-1s, and a more health-wary consumer, are more than offsetting other tailwinds.

Those industry limitations are almost impossible to overcome, which means Nestle and its peers are struggling to keep up with parts of the economy that are growing, and much faster. As a result, the consumer staples sector has been a huge underperformer over the past decade, and aside from short risk-off periods, there hasn't really been a moment they outperformed.

Nestle's case is even tougher, being the largest company already. That, combined with several ill-advised acquisitions, aggressive price increases, and less investing in order to preserve margins, have all led Nestle to even worse performance, and flat sales.

All those reasons are why I've been on the sidelines with Nestle for quite some time, and encouraged investors to allocate their money elsewhere.

Now, with the new CEO, some investors are hoping for a change.

Capping Off Another Flat Year

Before we discuss the new CEO's plan, let's briefly go over where he's starting, as he took over the helms back in August 2024.

In 2024, Nestle sales were CHF 91.3 billion, a 1.8% decline from 2023, marking the second year in a row of declining revenues.

Nestle and many other large staple companies like to focus on organic growth, as it excludes FX headwinds, acquisitions, and disposals. I'd encourage investors to be cautious on overly relying on these metrics. At the end of the day, these are global companies and FX headwinds are part of the business.

Anyways, in 2024, organic growth was 2.2%, of which 1.5% is attributed to pricing, and 0.8% is attributed to real internal growth, which is a metric that counts mix and volume.

While gross margins improved, operating margins declined, as Nestle tries to fuel growth with investments, specifically in marketing. Net profit dropped 2.9%, and EPS was down 1%.

Looking at the separate categories, we note that most of the growing categories saw margin declines, with Petcare and Health Science being the outliers. Elsewhere, Prepared Dishes and Milk Products are the only declining categories in 2024, but they do represent a meaningful portion of sales, with 23%.

Geographically, the only region with negative organic growth was North America. However, on a reported basis, all regions were negative.

So, I think that Nestle results fully align with the view that its growth opportunities are limited.

There could be some trade-offs between margins and growth, and some individual categories are stronger than others, but as a whole, there's not much to be excited about right now.

New CEO's Plan - Faster Growth, Lower Margins

Nestle's new CEO has a very solid, almost flawless plan, in my view. This is exactly what I would expect from a new CEO, and what I'd assume an activist investor in Nestle would push for.

He wants to focus on operational efficiencies, cut weaker brands, and utilize the savings to increase investments and focus on winners. This, hopefully, can bring back Nestle to real earnings growth.

They quantified the savings opportunity at CHF 2.5 billion, to gradually materialize over the next four years. Of which, the majority of savings will come from better procurement, followed by operational efficiencies, and commercial investments.

By cutting out struggling brands, increasing advertising and expanding winners, they seek to get Nestle over the 4% organic growth threshold. They have a more detailed plan for each category, which is outside the scope of this article.

And just like most transformation plans, the new management team is setting a low bar for their first year. In 2025, they guided for improved growth compared to the underwhelming 2.2% in 2024. That will come at a lower margin, as they invest for growth.

In the medium term, they want to achieve a formula of 4%+ organic growth, and 17%+ margins. It's interesting because on the one hand this goal seems to be quite ambitious, and on the other hand it doesn't seem that impressive either.

Nestle's Valuation Doesn't Leave Room For Upside

I've been saying for a long time that valuations in the consumer staples category make little sense. How do companies with no apparent growth opportunities trade at multiples that are far above the market average?

The answer that I kept getting was that they are safer, resilient names, that allow investors to "sleep well at night". I don't know about you, but I wouldn't be sleeping well at night with a 120% underperformance compared to the S&P 500 over the past decade.

I'd argue just investing in the index would make you sleep much better at night anyways.

Now, the sector did see significant declines over the past couple of years, and some names are now trading below market levels. Nestle is one of them.

At 19 times forward earnings, Nestle is leading the middle pack which includes names like Kimberly-Clark (KMB), PepsiCo (PEP), and Unilever (UL), all of which are similarly struggling companies in terms of growth prospects.

With that in mind, my view is that unlike previous articles, Nestle is finally at a reasonable valuation.

Conclusion

The consumer staples category has been struggling to find growth in the years that followed the pandemic. Nestle was no different, with two consecutive years of revenue declines.

For a long time, staple companies, Nestle included, were trading at above-market multiples in the mid-20s, making them very unattractive investments.

With Nestle now at 19 times forward earnings, the valuation makes more sense. That said, even if the new CEO's plan succeeds, Nestle would still be a low-growth company, as low-single-digit organic growth, combined with lower margins, would likely translate to little to no profit growth.

Therefore, I reiterate a 'Hold' for Nestle, but unlike previous articles, I don't see reason to encourage investors to sell, and can support owning it in a low-risk portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Invest In A New Biotech Bull Market With XBI And IBB
data.description:
Biotech Is Oversold… Again!

Ever since the biotech peak in February 2021, the rolling bear market has had a series of periods of extreme pain. There have been several bounces off bottoms in the past few years, but none have lasted long enough to start another bull market. I believe in capital cycles which means when too much capital chases a hot industry, it leads to low returns. When capital runs away from an industry, it’s left with only the strongest survivors. After a few years of outflows, opportunities to deploy capital in high returning projects increases.

As you can see from the chart below, we’ve been in the mother of all bear markets in the past four years. The declines have been so sharp that the IBB (NASDAQ:IBB) and XBI (NYSEARCA:XBI) ETFs are up 3.3% and down 1.7% since July 20th, 2015. That’s about 9.5 years of no returns. It’s a true-lost decade. The longer this goes on, the more likely we are to see a sustained strong bull market. Therefore, the likely bounce we get in the next few months might be the start of a new bull run.

Based on my experience, when we see really dire statistics, a near-term bottom is close. I acknowledge I’m using my judgment, but I have a lot of stats to back up how bad things are. Yes, they can get worse in the next few weeks, but I think we’ll see recovery within the next few months at the latest. Specifically, Seeking Alpha grades IBB’s momentum as a D- and XBI’s momentum as an F. Furthermore, XBI’s RSI fell to 35.6 on February 27th. XBI fell to within 1 point of its 6-month low.

Now, let’s look at some stats from the industry as a whole. As of February 27th, 42% of biotech stocks were down 50% or more in the past 12 months. As of the 25th, 36% of biotech stocks were down over 20% year to date. That’s crazy because the year is only two months old. The situation is dire for small biotech stocks. Also from the 25th, median year to date returns for biotech nano caps, micro caps, and small caps were -19.4%, -11.9%, and -11.6%. These small firms are mostly in the clinical stage, which explains their weakness. The BBC (BBC) clinical stage biotech ETF is down 15% year to date and 32.4% in the past year.

Sometimes the worst performers in the previous year recover following tax loss selling at year-end. That hasn’t happened. Smaller companies do the worst in the flight to safety trade. This panic trade won’t continue at this rate for much longer.

IBB Vs. XBI: I Like Both

Since I’m bullish on US biotech stocks in the short term (3-6 months) and the long-term (3-5 years), it’s not surprising that I’m bullish on both IBB and XBI which are US biotech ETFs. I like them for different reasons. Let’s review the differences first.

Even though XBI is known for its greater exposure to small caps because it’s equal-weighted, IBB has more stocks in it. IBB has 261 holdings and XBI has 141 holdings. IBB holds one of my two favorite micro-cap biotech stocks called Larimar Therapeutics (LRMR) (a 0.01% weighting). While IBB has more stocks, its top stocks have a much higher weighting, because its market cap weighted. The top four holdings are 34.72% of the index. Regeneron (REGN) has a 7.31% weighting. I have owned this stock previously; I like the management team and think the stock is undervalued. The top four holdings in XBI are only 11.14% of the index. Regeneron only has a 2.25% weighting.

XBI’s higher exposure to smaller firms is why it’s more volatile than IBB. That works well in bull markets and very poorly in bear markets. It’s exposure to small firms explains why it’s down 1.5% year to date which is way worse than IBB’s 3.7% increase. IBB is partially benefitting from the flight to safety rally in healthcare stocks this year. Large and mid-cap biotech stocks are among the safest in the market because they have high margins and aren’t cyclical. Clinical stage micro-cap biotech stocks are among the riskiest due to their high burn rates and approval risk. Both do well in recessions usually. Large caps do well because investors flee cyclical stocks for non-cyclical healthcare stocks. Micro-caps do well because long-term treasury yields fall.

IBB is great for investors who fear an overvalued market, the risk of a tech correction, and a potential recession. It does that while also providing some upside in case clinical-stage biotech stocks finally turn around. XBI is good for investors who are more confident in the clinical-stage biotech turnaround, but don’t want to research individual stocks. I allocated my family’s funds to IBB. Personally, I like to pick stocks, so I’m long Abeona Therapeutics (ABEO) (not in either) and Larimar Therapeutics (in IBB not XBI).

Macro Environment Ripe For Biotech Outperformance

It’s particularly disheartening to see the small cap biotech crash in 2025 because rates have been falling. The 10-year yield is down from 4.58% to 4.22% this year. We can’t even blame high rates for the rout this time. I’m not rooting for a recession, but lower inflation and weak economic growth are good for biotech. The Truflation index has inflation at 1.51% which is its lowest reading since January 2021. The Cleveland Fed Nowcast shows headline CPI will be 2.83% and headline PCE inflation will be 2.39% in February.

The CME Group FedWatch tool shows there is an 80.4% chance of at least two rate cuts this year (50 basis points). As you can see below, the Atlanta Fed GDP Nowcast suggests Q1 GDP growth will be -1.5%. I expect it to bounce slightly once all the data comes in, but the economy certainly isn’t scorching hot. All these macro stats suggest biotech stocks should be doing fine. I’ll dig deeper to find the fundamental issues.

Capital Should Flow To Biotech If AI/Crypto Rout Continues

I believe once the hype surrounding AI tech stocks and cryptocurrencies wane, investors will put speculative money into biotech stocks. We’ve seen cracks in AI stocks ever since the DeepSeek news came out. Nvidia is down 16.2% from its record close. Investors are skeptical of the hyperscalers’ capex spending to build out AI infrastructure. Data centers might not have a good return on investment. This goes back to the capital cycle theory. In this case, far too much capital is chasing after the same industry which will lead to low future returns. We’ve also seen bitcoin and altcoins correct. Bitcoin and Solana are down 23% and 51% from their record highs as of February 28th.

Some believe biotech stocks have too high of a barrier to entry for generalist investors. Therefore, if money leaves AI and crypto, they think it won’t come to biotech. I disagree. I believe investors will buy biotech stocks if they gain momentum and have strong earnings growth. Ideally, when you pick individual stocks, you are buying the ones that will drive this earnings growth. Another critical point is if we have a recession and investors can’t buy tech stocks because the AI hype is waning, they will have few choices outside of healthcare and biotech stocks. Even if we don’t get a recession and just have a low growth economy, it would still be a great situation for biotech stocks.

Capital Cycle Signals A Bottom

I’m not going to argue it’s impossible to raise funds if you are a biotech startup with an amazing new technology. The best firms can still get funding. However, the types of firms that probably shouldn’t have gotten funding in 2021 are no longer getting money. As you can see from the charts below, venture investments have declined dramatically in the past few years and there have been fewer R&D licensees.

Biotech firms take a long time to commercialize their technology. It can take well over 10 years to get an FDA approval. Therefore, some of the IPOs from 2020-2021 are still going, dragging down the nano/microcap performance I cited earlier. With that being said, the baby has been thrown out with the bathwater. I’ve seen plenty of biotech stocks with strong prospects also crashing, such as my holdings.

The bubble in biotech in 2020 and 2021 was caused by optimism related to Covid-19 treatments along with low rates and a rising market in speculative stocks. Biotech hasn’t participated at all in the bull run from 2023 to early 2025 which is arguably more euphoric. As you can see below, the number of biotech IPOs spiked from 43 to 99 from 2019 to 2021. The total money raised crashed from $15.6 billion in 2021 to just $2.5 billion in 2022. The IPO market has improved a bit, but it’s not back to where it was in 2019 (2024 data goes up to December 9th).

The good news is biotech stocks need to rise before we see a spike in IPOs which will dilute the market. Usually, only the strongest firms go public in bear markets, meaning the current landscape is quite strong outside of the firms that went public 4-5 years ago. Many of those firms are zombies nearly running out of cash. Once the worst firms from the prior bubble go away, the capital cycle restarts. As you can see from the graphic below, IPOs from weak companies caused a very low percentage of firms to have positive phase 2 and phase 3 readouts in 2021 and 2022. Results got better in 2023 & 1H 2024, but have gotten worse again recently.

As usual, the bear market is a mixture of terrible sentiment and weak fundamentals. If you pick stocks that get approvals and grow sales profitably, you will do well outside of the routs like we have seen recently. I believe we will get better phase 2 and phase 3 readouts in 2025 since many of the weak companies that went public 4-5 years ago have run out of money. As you can see below, globally there went from 892 publicly traded biotech firms in May 2022 to 758 at year-end 2024 which is a 15% decline.

I’m Fading The Trump Trades + FDA START Program

This brings us to the FDA since regulators play a part in how well these stocks do. While the quality of the drugs and data are the main drivers of stock performance, regulatory clarity is also very important. I’m fading all the ‘Trump trades.’ I don’t think the supposed beneficiaries of Trump’s presidency, namely crypto and Tesla, will be helped by his presidency as much as investors hoped in November. Similarly, I don’t expect his election to hurt biotech & medical device firms as much as people fear.

My thesis was supported by the recent rehiring of the medical device regulators at the FDA that were briefly fired. The reversal occurred after medical industry executives complained. I don’t believe the PDUFA (Prescription Drug User Fee Act), which lets companies pay to speed up their regulatory decisions, will be reversed. That’s because the alternative is government funding which conservatives generally dislike. There might be some changes to how food and supplements are regulated, but I don’t expect major changes to drug and device regulations.

I’m bullish on the FDA’s START program (Support for clinical Trials Advancing Rare disease Therapeutics) which Larimar was included in. START encourages an open dialogue between the FDA and biotech firms to make sure both are on the same page. It saves a lot of time and headaches if the FDA tells biotech firms what they need to do throughout the process instead of surprising them on decision day after not talking for a while.

US Needs Better Treatments To Save Money

New drugs save the healthcare system money even if they are priced highly. The best way to lower costs is to treat diseases better, shortening hospital stays. For example, Abeona Therapeutics’ pz-cel treatment for Recessive Dystrophic Epidermolysis Bullosa ("RDEB") costs $1.5 million, but it saves the system a lot of money on bandages and time in the hospital by properly treating patients’ wounds. No patients treated with pz-cel have developed Squamous Cell Carcinoma ("SCC") which is common in RDEB patients.

As you can see from the chart below, healthcare expenditures are set to approach 20% of the US economy in the early 2030s. Costs are rising due to the aging population. There’s no question the healthcare system can be reformed to cut costs. New drugs and medical devices are the easiest ways to save money because the government doesn’t have to do anything. Biotech and medical device firms do the hard work. This is much more politically viable than lowering benefits.

Huge Patent Cliff & Cash Hoard Signals An Uptick In M&A

Small cap biotech firms are major beneficiaries of M&A. More deals will help the small companies in XBI and IBB get premium valuations and the large acquiring firms bring back growth. For example, Regeneron’s main drug Eylea faces biosimilar competition. It can use its $17.9 billion in cash (as of Q4 2024) to buy small biotech firms to help make up for this decline. Smart acquisitions could help shares more than buybacks and dividends. This is just one example of a large cap biotech firm with excess cash and a need to refill its pipeline. As you can see below, AbbVie’s (ABBV) Humira lost its patent protection, which put its $21.2 billion business in free fall (AbbVie is the second-largest position in XBI, but it’s not in IBB).

According to William Blair, from 2023 through 2025, 50 drugs will lose patent protection, which will cause their total sales to fall from $162.8 billion in 2025 to $67 billion in 2029. This is while top pharma firms had over $170 billion in cash in mid-2024. They have the money to spend on acquisitions and the need for growth, which should help M&A recover in the coming years.

Where I Could Be Wrong

It’s possible we won’t see a return to M&A even though many large pharmaceutical firms need to make up for their patent cliffs. As you can see from the chart below, there were only 18 deals worth just $5.9 billion in Q4 2024, which was the lowest since Q1 2022. Maybe a large swath of biotech firms are going after the wrong treatment modalities. Even though biotech is in a bear market, there are some hot areas which might miss their targets. For example, COVID-19 created false hype for mRNA vaccines. Moderna’s stock has crashed so hard, it’s only up 50% from January 2021, which was before the pandemic. The current hot trend is weight loss/muscle gain drugs due to GLP-1’s success.

It’s very difficult to project the results of an entire industry because I am not individually following all 700+ biotech firms to see if their science is sound and their trial results are positive.
