The fact that there are still so many publicly traded biotech firms compared to decades ago could mean we still have years of seeing zombie companies fail before we start a new bull run.

While the Trump administration won’t make major regulatory changes, uncertainty could lengthen approval times. The Trump administration could halt the START program I’m so bullish on. If the government cuts taxes and regulations, it could create a hot economy with high rates and inflation, which would be bad for biotech valuations.

data.title: KIO: High Yield Fund, Fully Covered Distribution, Currently Yielding 10% Plus
data.description:
Introduction:

KKR Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:KIO) is a closed-end fund that was incepted in Jul. 2013. The fund invests in high-conviction leveraged loans, high-yield bonds, and structured credit with a flexible mandate designed to take advantage of market dislocations. It has the advantage of leveraging the differentiated sourcing capabilities of KKR (the parent group) to capture attractive investment opportunities. The fund offers the following attractive features to fixed-income investors: 1) Potential for attractive levels of high income through monthly distributions 2) A targeted and diversified fixed-income portfolio investing primarily in bank loans and high-yield securities 3) A consistent approach and strong credit investment process that seeks to adapt credit strategies to market conditions.

As of Dec. 31, 2024, the portfolio composition was as presented below.

Chart-1:

As per the fund's literature, "KKR Income Opportunities Fund is a diversified, closed-end management investment company. The Fund's primary investment objective is to seek a high level of current income with a secondary objective of capital appreciation. The fund seeks to allocate across credit instruments to capitalize on changes in relative value among corporate credit investments and manage against macroeconomic risks."

Other salient features of this fund are as follows:

  • The Fund is managed by KKR Credit Advisors, LLC, which serves as the Fund's investment adviser. Launched in 2004, the Adviser is a subsidiary of KKR & Co. Inc., one of the leading global investment firms with more than a 40-year history behind it.
  • The fund's main objective is to provide its shareholders with a high level of current income, distributed monthly. The fund's investment strategy has a focus on generating a high level of income and capital preservation.
  • The fund has a reasonably long history of over 11 years.
  • The fund uses significant leverage, typically in the range of 35%, which includes the preferred shares. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the leverage used excluding the preferred shares stood at 26.13%, but after including the preferred shares, it amounted to 35.15%.
  • The fund charges roughly a 1.70% management fee. Due to high leverage, the interest expenses account for roughly 3.09% (based on 2024 figures, including the interest for preferred shares). The total expenses (including advisory fee, interest, and other misc. expenses) accounted for roughly 5.32% based on net assets and 3.38% on total assets.
  • As of Dec. 31, 2024, the fund had roughly $555 million of AUM (total assets under management). The net assets stood at $360 million, while the preferred shares were equivalent to $50 million and leverage at $145 million.
  • As of Feb.26, 2025, KIO's forward distribution yield stands at 11.61%, based on its regular monthly distribution. The distribution has been quite consistent and was last increased in early 2023. The current discount to its NAV stands at -4.34%.

Who should invest in KIO?

  • For most years, the fund KIO has been able to fully cover its distributions by net investment income. However, the NAV can grow or shrink based on the appreciation or depreciation of the underlying assets. The value of the underlying assets depends on the perceived value that the market may assign based on a wide range of factors. So, just because the fund currently provides an 11.6% yield, it does not mean that the overall annual return will be similar. The annualized returns will depend upon what price we buy and sell. So, that's why in funds like this, it is important to not overpay and preferably buy when an attractive discount is available (in relation to historical premium/discount).
  • From chart-1 above (from Dec. 31, 2024), it appears that the fund is almost equally allocated to floating-rate instruments (leveraged loans) and high-yield securities (mostly junk bonds that are fixed-rate instruments). In a declining interest rate environment, the market value of floating rate instruments will generally decline. With inflation and interest rate direction somewhat hazy at this time and can change due to many factors, it is difficult to predict the direction of this fund.
  • At the current price and the discount, we think that the fund is neither cheap nor expensive. So, we can call it fairly priced. The current yield is attractive, but we feel the fund is somewhat overpaying the distribution, and we should not expect more than 8% to 9% annualized returns in the long term, which is not bad for a fixed-income fund. So, overall, it is a decent investment at this time for high-income investors who can wait patiently for many years while collecting the income. There is a slight risk of dividend reduction, and such an event can also impact market price.

Financial Outlook:

Let's look at the Fund's Financial health and performance. The most recent annual report that the fund has released is for the period from Nov. 2023 to Oct. 31, 2024.

Net Investment Income:

The net investment income (or NII in short) is the net income that a fund earns from its investments in the form of dividends, distributions, interests, and any other generated income (for example, buy-write options) minus all of the fund's expenses, including management fees, operating expenses, commissions, and interest on leverage, if any. For equity-based funds, especially in high-growth sectors like technology, the NII is not much relevant compared to fixed-income funds.

Here is what it looks like in terms of NII, Distributions, and Net Assets at the beginning and end of the statement period.

(all amounts are in US $ (except Shares Outstanding); negative amounts are shown inside parentheses, per the 12-month Annual report, fiscal year ending Oct. 31, 2024, and the prior period ending Oct.31, 2023). The last three digits are rounded for all amount figures.

Table-1:

Distributions:

The fund follows a 'managed' distribution policy. It currently pays $0.1215 a share on a monthly basis, which works out to be a yield of 11.61% on current prices as of Feb.26, 2025. The last time it increased the dividend payout was in Mar. 2023, from $0.1050 to $.1215 a share (a 15.71% increase). Prior to that, the payout was decreased in Nov. 2020 by 16%.

So, is the distribution covered?

Since this is a fixed-income fund, it is very important that the fund fully or nearly covers the distributions by its net investment income. The NII (net investment Income) is the net income that the fund earns after subtracting all expenses, including interest, advisory fees, and any other misc expenses, from the total investment income it generates from interests and dividends. On the basis of NII, the distributions of this fund are fully covered. The fund has not paid any distributions from ROC (return of capital) since 2020 that we could verify. We can see in the below table the coverage ratio for fiscal years from 2020-2024. We have also provided the totals for 5 years in column-2.

We have discussed the coverage, but what about the NAV (net asset value)? The fund also makes some realized and unrealized appreciation/depreciation (capital gains or losses). So, if we see the net totals (surplus or deficit) from investment operations (row no.6), we can see if the NAV is growing or shrinking. The NAV can grow or shrink for many reasons, depending upon the cycle that we are in. This is because the valuations of the underlying assets can go up or down based on how the market perceives the value. On a long-term basis, for a fund like this with an 11.5% yield, we want the NAV to be stable, if not growing.

Discount/Premium:

The fund is currently trading at a relatively small discount of just about -4.34% (to NAV). However, it traded at a premium for the second half of 2024, with the premium going as high as 15% in Oct. 2024. However, if we look at the long-term averages, it has traded mostly at an average discount of -4% to -6%. So, in that sense, the current discount is mostly in line with the long-term averages.

That said, discount/premium relative to mid-term and long-term averages is only one of the important factors that we should look at.

Chart-2A: Premium/Discount information (Past 1 year)

Chart-2B: Premium/Discount information (Past 5 years)

Funds Holdings/Composition:

Here, we look at the fund's portfolio in terms of what it holds (as of Dec. 31, 2024). These are obviously not our typical equity holdings; rather, they are fixed-income securities, in fact, mostly bank loans (leveraged loans) and high-yield corporate bond holdings. The majority of holdings have below-investment-grade ratings. The fund is fairly diversified with nearly 250 holdings, and the top-10 account for nearly 13%.

Here is the performance of the fund KIO, which is presented on the fund's factsheet.

From the performance perspective, the performance for most periods is comparable to other similar funds, is lackluster. The fund ARDC does slightly better in the short term (last 2 or 3 years), but in the long term, it has similar performance. Also, both KIO and ARDC fully cover the distribution by NII. But please note that ARDC reduced its distribution just last month by about 4.25%. We also combined two BlackRock funds to make one hybrid fund that would have a similar portfolio composition. Again, we found that KIO does pretty well in comparison with this hybrid fund.

Risk Factors:

Investors need to be aware of certain risk factors that are associated with this fund and CEFs in general. Risk factors could be summarized as follows:

  • Leverage risk: The fund uses significant leverage (roughly 35%) and pays a substantial amount in interest. This is especially negative during periods when the interest rates are high. Also, due to leverage, both gains and losses that a fund may accrue will be magnified due to leverage.
  • General market risk: The fund is not invested in equities but rather in high-yield (below investment grade) corporate bonds and leveraged loans. As such, its investment income should not be impacted by the ups and downs of the stock market, but during deep corrections (or recessions), the expectations of loan default rates would typically increase. In such a situation, the market value of the fund's holdings could take a dive, impacting the NAV significantly.
  • Geo-political risk: The geo-political situation remains volatile around the world.
  • Risk of recession: The possibility of a recession in the future cannot be ruled out, though the risk appears to be low at this point. During deep recessions, loan default rates can rise, negatively impacting the NAV of the fund.
  • Higher Risk from High-Yield Bonds: The Fund invests in high-yield bonds, also known as "junk bonds," including some distressed securities. All of these securities are subject to greater credit risk (the risk that an issuer won't make timely payments of principal and/or interest).

Concluding Thoughts:

We believe this fund is fairly priced, but it can still get cheaper in the coming months as the trend has been down. Its price and discount movement will also depend on the direction of interest rates. There is a low risk of dividend reduction, but any such reduction should be minor.

This fund can be used as a diversification into fixed-income and high-yield asset classes. We think existing buyers should hold it and keep an eye on distribution coverage. The new buyers could wait for a better discount to appear or accumulate it over a period of time using dollar-cost-averaging.
